solution:
\textbf{First solution.}
Since fixed points do not affect the signature of a permutation, we may
ignore the residue class of $0$ and consider $\pi$ as a permutation on the
nonzero residue classes modulo $p$. These form a cyclic group of order $p-1$,
so the signature of $\pi$ is also the signature of multiplication by 3 as
a permutation $\sigma$ of the residue classes modulo $p-1$. If we identify
these classes with the integers $0,\dots,p-2$, then the signature equals
the parity of the number of \emph{inversions}: these are the pairs $(i,j)$
with $0 \leq i < j \leq p-2$ for which $\sigma(i) > \sigma(j)$. We may write
\[
\sigma(i) = 3i - (p-1) \left\lfloor \frac{3i}{p-1} \right\rfloor
\]
from which we see that $(i,j)$ cannot be an inversion unless $\lfloor \frac{3j}{p-1} \rfloor > \lfloor \frac{3i}{p-1} \rfloor$. In particular, we only obtain inversions when $i < 2(p-1)/3$.

If $i < (p-1)/3$, the elements $j$ of $\{0,\dots,p-2\}$ for which $(i,j)$ is an inversion correspond to the elements of $\{0,\dots,3i\}$ which are not multiples of $3$, which are $2i$ in number.
This contributes a total of $0 + 2 + \cdots + 2(p-2)/3 = (p-2)(p+1)/9$ inversions.

If $(p-1)/3 < i < 2(p-1)/3$, the elements $j$ of $\{0,\dots,p-2\}$ for which $(i,j)$ is an inversion correspond
to the elements of $\{0, \dots, 3i-p+1\}$ congruent to 1 modulo 3, which are
$(3i-p+2)/3 = i - (p-2)/3$ in number. This contributes a total of
$1 + \cdots + (p-2)/3 = (p-2)(p+1)/18$ inversions.

Summing up, the total number of inversions is $(p-2)(p+1)/6$, which is even if and only if
$p \equiv 3 \pmod{4}$. This proves the claim.

\noindent
\textbf{Second solution} (by Noam Elkies).
Recall that the sign of $\pi$ (which is $+1$ if $\pi$ is even and $-1$ if $\pi$ is odd)
can be computed as
\[
\prod_{0 \leq x < y < p} \frac{\pi(x) - \pi(y)}{x - y}
\]
(because composing $\pi$ with a transposition changes the sign of the product).
Reducing modulo $p$, we get a congruence with
\[
\prod_{0 \leq x < y < p} \frac{x^3-y^3}{x-y} = \prod_{0 \leq x < y < p} (x^2 + xy + y^2).
\]
It thus suffices to count the number of times each possible value of $x^2+xy+y^2$ occurs.
Each nonzero value $c$ modulo $p$ occurs $p+1$ times as $x^2+xy+y^2$ with $0 \leq x, y < p$
and hence $(p + \chi(c/3))/2$ times with $0 \leq x < y < p$, where $\chi$ denotes the quadratic character
modulo $p$. Since $p \equiv 2 \pmod{3}$, by the law of quadratic reciprocity we have
$\chi(-3) = +1$, so $\chi(c/3) = \chi(-c)$.
It thus remains to evaluate the product $\prod_{c=1}^{p-1} c^{(p+\chi(-c))/2}$ modulo $p$.

If $p \equiv 3 \pmod{4}$, this is easy: each factor is a quadratic residue (this is clear if $c$ is a residue,
and otherwise $\chi(-c) = +1$ so $p+\chi(-c)$ is divisible by $4$) and $-1$ is not, so we must get $+1$
modulo $p$.

If $p \equiv 1 \pmod{4}$, we must do more work: we
choose a primitive root $g$ modulo $p$ and rewrite the product as
\[
\prod_{i=0}^{p-2} g^{i(p+(-1)^i)/2}.
\]
The sum of the exponents, split into sums over $i$ odd and $i$ even, gives
\[
\sum_{j=0}^{(p-3)/2} \left( j(p+1) + \frac{(2j+1)(p-1)}{2}\right)
\]
which simplifies to
\[
\frac{(p-3)(p-1)(p+1)}{8} + \frac{(p-1)^3}{8} = \frac{p-1}{2} \left( \frac{p^2 - 1}{2} - p \right).
\]
Hence the product we are trying to evaluate is congruent to $g^{(p-1)/2} \equiv -1$ modulo $p$.

\noindent
\textbf{Third solution} (by Mark van Hoeij).
We compute the parity of $\pi$ as the parity of the number of cycles of even length
in the cycle decomposition of $\pi$.
For $x$ a nonzero residue class modulo $p$ of multiplicative order $d$, the elements of the orbit of $x$
under $\pi$ also have order $d$ (because $d$ divides $p-1$ and hence is coprime to $3$). Since the group
of nonzero residue classes modulo $p$ is cyclic of order $p-1$, the elements of order $d$ fall into
$\varphi(d)/f(d)$ orbits under $\pi$, where $\varphi$ is the Euler phi function and $f(d)$ is the
multiplicative order of 3 modulo $d$. The parity of $\pi$ is then the parity of the sum of $\varphi(d)/f(d)$
over all divisors $d$ of $p-1$ for which $f(d)$ is even.

If $d$ is odd, then $\varphi(d)/f(d) = \varphi(2d)/f(2d)$, so the summands corresponding to $d$ and $2d$
coincide. It thus suffices to consider those $d$ divisible by $4$. If $p \equiv 3 \pmod{4}$, then there are no
such summands, so the sum is trivially even.

If $p \equiv 1 \pmod{4}$, then $d=4$ contributes a summand of $\varphi(4)/f(4) = 2/2 = 1$.
For each $d$ which is a larger multiple of 4, the group $(\ZZ/d\ZZ)^*$ is isomorphic to the product of
$\ZZ/2\ZZ$ with another group of even order, so the maximal power of 2 dividing $f(d)$ is strictly smaller
than the maximal power of 2 dividing $d$. Hence $\varphi(d)/f(d)$ is even, and so the overall sum is odd.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark.}
Note that the second proof uses quadratic reciprocity, whereas the first and third proofs are similar to several classical
proofs of quadratic reciprocity. Abhinav Kumar notes that the problem itself is a special case
of the Duke-Hopkins quadratic reciprocity law for abelian groups (Quadratic reciprocity in a finite group,
\textit{Amer. Math. Monthly} \textbf{112} (2005), 251--256; see also
\url{http://math.uga.edu/~pete/morequadrec.pdf}).


\end{itemize}
\end{document}

descriptive_long.question:
Let $primebase$ be an odd prime number such that $primebase \equiv 2 \pmod{3}$. Define a permutation $permutpi$ of the
residue classes modulo $primebase$ by $permutpi(firstvar) \equiv firstvar^3 \pmod{primebase}$. Show that $permutpi$ is an even permutation
if and only if $primebase \equiv 3 \pmod{4}$.
descriptive_long.solution:
\textbf{First solution.}
Since fixed points do not affect the signature of a permutation, we may
ignore the residue class of $0$ and consider $permutpi$ as a permutation on the
nonzero residue classes modulo $primebase$. These form a cyclic group of order $primebase-1$,
so the signature of $permutpi$ is also the signature of multiplication by 3 as
a permutation $multperm$ of the residue classes modulo $primebase-1$. If we identify
these classes with the integers $0,\dots,primebase-2$, then the signature equals
the parity of the number of \emph{inversions}: these are the pairs $(firstindex,secondindex)$
with $0 \leq firstindex < secondindex \leq primebase-2$ for which $multperm(firstindex) > multperm(secondindex)$. We may write
\[
multperm(firstindex) = 3 firstindex - (primebase-1) \left\lfloor \frac{3 firstindex}{primebase-1} \right\rfloor
\]
from which we see that $(firstindex,secondindex)$ cannot be an inversion unless $\lfloor \tfrac{3 secondindex}{primebase-1} \rfloor > \lfloor \tfrac{3 firstindex}{primebase-1} \rfloor$. In particular, we only obtain inversions when $firstindex < 2(primebase-1)/3$.

If $firstindex < (primebase-1)/3$, the elements $secondindex$ of $\{0,\dots,primebase-2\}$ for which $(firstindex,secondindex)$ is an inversion correspond to the elements of $\{0,\dots,3 firstindex\}$ which are not multiples of $3$, which are $2 firstindex$ in number.
This contributes a total of $0 + 2 + \cdots + 2(primebase-2)/3 = (primebase-2)(primebase+1)/9$ inversions.

If $(primebase-1)/3 < firstindex < 2(primebase-1)/3$, the elements $secondindex$ of $\{0,\dots,primebase-2\}$ for which $(firstindex,secondindex)$ is an inversion correspond
to the elements of $\{0, \dots, 3 firstindex-primebase+1\}$ congruent to 1 modulo 3, which are
$(3 firstindex-primebase+2)/3 = firstindex - (primebase-2)/3$ in number. This contributes a total of
$1 + \cdots + (primebase-2)/3 = (primebase-2)(primebase+1)/18$ inversions.

Summing up, the total number of inversions is $(primebase-2)(primebase+1)/6$, which is even if and only if
$primebase \equiv 3 \pmod{4}$. This proves the claim.

\noindent
\textbf{Second solution} (by Noam Elkies).
Recall that the sign of $permutpi$ (which is $+1$ if $permutpi$ is even and $-1$ if $permutpi$ is odd)
can be computed as
\[
\prod_{0 \leq firstvar < secondvar < primebase} \frac{permutpi(firstvar) - permutpi(secondvar)}{firstvar - secondvar}
\]
(because composing $permutpi$ with a transposition changes the sign of the product).
Reducing modulo $primebase$, we get a congruence with
\[
\prod_{0 \leq firstvar < secondvar < primebase} \frac{firstvar^3-secondvar^3}{firstvar-secondvar} = \prod_{0 \leq firstvar < secondvar < primebase} (firstvar^2 + firstvar secondvar + secondvar^2).
\]
It thus suffices to count the number of times each possible value of $firstvar^2+firstvar secondvar+secondvar^2$ occurs.
Each nonzero value $alphaconst$ modulo $primebase$ occurs $primebase+1$ times as $firstvar^2+firstvar secondvar+secondvar^2$ with $0 \leq firstvar, secondvar < primebase$
and hence $(primebase + quadchar(alphaconst/3))/2$ times with $0 \leq firstvar < secondvar < primebase$, where $quadchar$ denotes the quadratic character
modulo $primebase$. Since $primebase \equiv 2 \pmod{3}$, by the law of quadratic reciprocity we have
$quadchar(-3) = +1$, so $quadchar(alphaconst/3) = quadchar(-alphaconst)$.
It thus remains to evaluate the product $\prod_{alphaconst=1}^{primebase-1} alphaconst^{(primebase+quadchar(-alphaconst))/2}$ modulo $primebase$.

If $primebase \equiv 3 \pmod{4}$, this is easy: each factor is a quadratic residue (this is clear if $alphaconst$ is a residue,
and otherwise $quadchar(-alphaconst) = +1$ so $primebase+quadchar(-alphaconst)$ is divisible by $4$) and $-1$ is not, so we must get $+1$
modulo $primebase$.

If $primebase \equiv 1 \pmod{4}$, we must do more work: we
choose a primitive root $primroot$ modulo $primebase$ and rewrite the product as
\[
\prod_{firstindex=0}^{primebase-2} primroot^{ firstindex(primebase+(-1)^{firstindex})/2 }.
\]
The sum of the exponents, split into sums over $firstindex$ odd and $firstindex$ even, gives
\[
\sum_{secondindex=0}^{(primebase-3)/2} \left( secondindex(primebase+1) + \frac{(2 secondindex+1)(primebase-1)}{2}\right)
\]
which simplifies to
\[
\frac{(primebase-3)(primebase-1)(primebase+1)}{8} + \frac{(primebase-1)^3}{8} = \frac{primebase-1}{2} \left( \frac{primebase^2 - 1}{2} - primebase \right).
\]
Hence the product we are trying to evaluate is congruent to $primroot^{(primebase-1)/2} \equiv -1$ modulo $primebase$.

\noindent
\textbf{Third solution} (by Mark van Hoeij).
We compute the parity of $permutpi$ as the parity of the number of cycles of even length
in the cycle decomposition of $permutpi$.
For $firstvar$ a nonzero residue class modulo $primebase$ of multiplicative order $ordermod$, the elements of the orbit of $firstvar$
under $permutpi$ also have order $ordermod$ (because $ordermod$ divides $primebase-1$ and hence is coprime to $3$). Since the group
of nonzero residue classes modulo $primebase$ is cyclic of order $primebase-1$, the elements of order $ordermod$ fall into
$eulerphi(ordermod)/orderthree(ordermod)$ orbits under $permutpi$, where $eulerphi$ is the Euler phi function and $orderthree(ordermod)$ is the
multiplicative order of 3 modulo $ordermod$. The parity of $permutpi$ is then the parity of the sum of $eulerphi(ordermod)/orderthree(ordermod)$
over all divisors $ordermod$ of $primebase-1$ for which $orderthree(ordermod)$ is even.

If $ordermod$ is odd, then $eulerphi(ordermod)/orderthree(ordermod) = eulerphi(2 ordermod)/orderthree(2 ordermod)$, so the summands corresponding to $ordermod$ and $2 ordermod$
coincide. It thus suffices to consider those $ordermod$ divisible by $4$. If $primebase \equiv 3 \pmod{4}$, then there are no
such summands, so the sum is trivially even.

If $primebase \equiv 1 \pmod{4}$, then $ordermod=4$ contributes a summand of $eulerphi(4)/orderthree(4) = 2/2 = 1$.
For each $ordermod$ which is a larger multiple of 4, the group $(\ZZ/ordermod\ZZ)^*$ is isomorphic to the product of
$\ZZ/2\ZZ$ with another group of even order, so the maximal power of 2 dividing $orderthree(ordermod)$ is strictly smaller
than the maximal power of 2 dividing $ordermod$. Hence $eulerphi(ordermod)/orderthree(ordermod)$ is even, and so the overall sum is odd.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark.}
Note that the second proof uses quadratic reciprocity, whereas the first and third proofs are similar to several classical
proofs of quadratic reciprocity. Abhinav Kumar notes that the problem itself is a special case
of the Duke-Hopkins quadratic reciprocity law for abelian groups (Quadratic reciprocity in a finite group,
\textit{Amer. Math. Monthly} \textbf{112} (2005), 251--256; see also
\url{http://math.uga.edu/~pete/morequadrec.pdf}).

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Let $blackbird$ be an odd prime number such that $blackbird \equiv 2 \pmod{3}$. Define a permutation $\pi$ of the
residue classes modulo $blackbird$ by $\pi(sailboat) \equiv sailboat^3 \pmod{blackbird}$. Show that $\pi$ is an even permutation
if and only if $blackbird \equiv 3 \pmod{4}$.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
\textbf{First solution.}
Since fixed points do not affect the signature of a permutation, we may
ignore the residue class of $0$ and consider $\pi$ as a permutation on the
nonzero residue classes modulo $blackbird$. These form a cyclic group of order $blackbird-1$,
so the signature of $\pi$ is also the signature of multiplication by 3 as
a permutation $semaphore$ of the residue classes modulo $blackbird-1$. If we identify
these classes with the integers $0,\dots,blackbird-2$, then the signature equals
the parity of the number of \emph{inversions}: these are the pairs $(marigold,pinecone)$
with $0 \leq marigold < pinecone \leq blackbird-2$ for which $semaphore(marigold) > semaphore(pinecone)$. We may write
\[
semaphore(marigold) = 3 marigold - (blackbird-1) \left\lfloor \frac{3 marigold}{blackbird-1} \right\rfloor
\]
from which we see that $(marigold,pinecone)$ cannot be an inversion unless $\left\lfloor \frac{3 pinecone}{blackbird-1} \right\rfloor > \left\lfloor \frac{3 marigold}{blackbird-1} \right\rfloor$. In particular, we only obtain inversions when $marigold < 2(blackbird-1)/3$.

If $marigold < (blackbird-1)/3$, the elements $pinecone$ of $\{0,\dots,blackbird-2\}$ for which $(marigold,pinecone)$ is an inversion correspond to the elements of $\{0,\dots,3 marigold\}$ which are not multiples of $3$, which are $2 marigold$ in number.
This contributes a total of $0 + 2 + \cdots + 2(blackbird-2)/3 = (blackbird-2)(blackbird+1)/9$ inversions.

If $(blackbird-1)/3 < marigold < 2(blackbird-1)/3$, the elements $pinecone$ of $\{0,\dots,blackbird-2\}$ for which $(marigold,pinecone)$ is an inversion correspond
to the elements of $\{0, \dots, 3 marigold-blackbird+1\}$ congruent to 1 modulo 3, which are
$(3 marigold-blackbird+2)/3 = marigold - (blackbird-2)/3$ in number. This contributes a total of
$1 + \cdots + (blackbird-2)/3 = (blackbird-2)(blackbird+1)/18$ inversions.

Summing up, the total number of inversions is $(blackbird-2)(blackbird+1)/6$, which is even if and only if
$blackbird \equiv 3 \pmod{4}$. This proves the claim.

\noindent
\textbf{Second solution} (by Noam Elkies).
Recall that the sign of $\pi$ (which is $+1$ if $\pi$ is even and $-1$ if $\pi$ is odd)
can be computed as
\[
\prod_{0 \leq sailboat < dovetail < blackbird} \frac{\pi(sailboat) - \pi(dovetail)}{sailboat - dovetail}
\]
(because composing $\pi$ with a transposition changes the sign of the product).
Reducing modulo $blackbird$, we get a congruence with
\[
\prod_{0 \leq sailboat < dovetail < blackbird} \frac{sailboat^3-dovetail^3}{sailboat-dovetail} = \prod_{0 \leq sailboat < dovetail < blackbird} (sailboat^2 + sailboat dovetail + dovetail^2).
\]
It thus suffices to count the number of times each possible value of $sailboat^2+sailboat dovetail+dovetail^2$ occurs.
Each nonzero value $chandelier$ modulo $blackbird$ occurs $blackbird+1$ times as $sailboat^2+sailboat dovetail+dovetail^2$ with $0 \leq sailboat, dovetail < blackbird$
and hence $(blackbird + lanterns(chandelier/3))/2$ times with $0 \leq sailboat < dovetail < blackbird$, where $lanterns$ denotes the quadratic character
modulo $blackbird$. Since $blackbird \equiv 2 \pmod{3}$, by the law of quadratic reciprocity we have
$lanterns(-3) = +1$, so $lanterns(chandelier/3) = lanterns(-chandelier)$.
It thus remains to evaluate the product $\prod_{chandelier=1}^{blackbird-1} chandelier^{(blackbird+lanterns(-chandelier))/2}$ modulo $blackbird$.

If $blackbird \equiv 3 \pmod{4}$, this is easy: each factor is a quadratic residue (this is clear if $chandelier$ is a residue,
and otherwise $lanterns(-chandelier) = +1$ so $blackbird+lanterns(-chandelier)$ is divisible by $4$) and $-1$ is not, so we must get $+1$
modulo $blackbird$.

If $blackbird \equiv 1 \pmod{4}$, we must do more work: we
choose a primitive root $drumstick$ modulo $blackbird$ and rewrite the product as
\[
\prod_{marigold=0}^{blackbird-2} drumstick^{marigold(blackbird+(-1)^{marigold})/2}.
\]
The sum of the exponents, split into sums over $marigold$ odd and $marigold$ even, gives
\[
\sum_{pinecone=0}^{(blackbird-3)/2} \left( pinecone(blackbird+1) + \frac{(2 pinecone+1)(blackbird-1)}{2}\right)
\]
which simplifies to
\[
\frac{(blackbird-3)(blackbird-1)(blackbird+1)}{8} + \frac{(blackbird-1)^3}{8} = \frac{blackbird-1}{2} \left( \frac{blackbird^2 - 1}{2} - blackbird \right).
\]
Hence the product we are trying to evaluate is congruent to $drumstick^{(blackbird-1)/2} \equiv -1$ modulo $blackbird$.

\noindent
\textbf{Third solution} (by Mark van Hoeij).
We compute the parity of $\pi$ as the parity of the number of cycles of even length
in the cycle decomposition of $\pi$.
For $sailboat$ a nonzero residue class modulo $blackbird$ of multiplicative order $toothpick$, the elements of the orbit of $sailboat$
under $\pi$ also have order $toothpick$ (because $toothpick$ divides $blackbird-1$ and hence is coprime to $3$). Since the group
of nonzero residue classes modulo $blackbird$ is cyclic of order $blackbird-1$, the elements of order $toothpick$ fall into
$harmonica(toothpick)/firestorm(toothpick)$ orbits under $\pi$, where $harmonica$ is the Euler phi function and $firestorm(toothpick)$ is the
multiplicative order of 3 modulo $toothpick$. The parity of $\pi$ is then the parity of the sum of $harmonica(toothpick)/firestorm(toothpick)$
over all divisors $toothpick$ of $blackbird-1$ for which $firestorm(toothpick)$ is even.

If $toothpick$ is odd, then $harmonica(toothpick)/firestorm(toothpick) = harmonica(2 toothpick)/firestorm(2 toothpick)$, so the summands corresponding to $toothpick$ and $2 toothpick$
coincide. It thus suffices to consider those $toothpick$ divisible by $4$. If $blackbird \equiv 3 \pmod{4}$, then there are no
such summands, so the sum is trivially even.

If $blackbird \equiv 1 \pmod{4}$, then $toothpick=4$ contributes a summand of $harmonica(4)/firestorm(4) = 2/2 = 1$.
For each $toothpick$ which is a larger multiple of 4, the group $(\ZZ/toothpick\ZZ)^*$ is isomorphic to the product of
$\ZZ/2\ZZ$ with another group of even order, so the maximal power of 2 dividing $firestorm(toothpick)$ is strictly smaller
than the maximal power of 2 dividing $toothpick$. Hence $harmonica(toothpick)/firestorm(toothpick)$ is even, and so the overall sum is odd.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark.}
Note that the second proof uses quadratic reciprocity, whereas the first and third proofs are similar to several classical
proofs of quadratic reciprocity. Abhinav Kumar notes that the problem itself is a special case
of the Duke-Hopkins quadratic reciprocity law for abelian groups (Quadratic reciprocity in a finite group,
\textit{Amer. Math. Monthly} \textbf{112} (2005), 251--256; see also
\url{http://math.uga.edu/~pete/morequadrec.pdf}).

descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
\textbf{First solution.}
Since fixed points do not affect the signature of a permutation, we may
ignore the residue class of $0$ and consider $stagnation$ as a permutation on the
nonzero residue classes modulo $compositenumber$. These form a cyclic group of order $compositenumber-1$,
so the signature of $stagnation$ is also the signature of multiplication by 3 as
a permutation $disorder$ of the residue classes modulo $compositenumber-1$. If we identify
these classes with the integers $0,\dots,compositenumber-2$, then the signature equals
the parity of the number of \emph{inversions}: these are the pairs $(totalvalue,aggregate)$
with $0 \leq totalvalue < aggregate \leq compositenumber-2$ for which $disorder(totalvalue) > disorder(aggregate)$. We may write
\[
disorder(totalvalue) = 3totalvalue - (compositenumber-1) \left\lfloor \frac{3totalvalue}{compositenumber-1} \right\rfloor
\]
from which we see that $(totalvalue,aggregate)$ cannot be an inversion unless $\lfloor \frac{3aggregate}{compositenumber-1} \rfloor > \lfloor \frac{3totalvalue}{compositenumber-1} \rfloor$. In particular, we only obtain inversions when $totalvalue < 2(compositenumber-1)/3$.

If $totalvalue < (compositenumber-1)/3$, the elements $aggregate$ of $\{0,\dots,compositenumber-2\}$ for which $(totalvalue,aggregate)$ is an inversion correspond to the elements of $\{0,\dots,3totalvalue\}$ which are not multiples of $3$, which are $2totalvalue$ in number.
This contributes a total of $0 + 2 + \cdots + 2(compositenumber-2)/3 = (compositenumber-2)(compositenumber+1)/9$ inversions.

If $(compositenumber-1)/3 < totalvalue < 2(compositenumber-1)/3$, the elements $aggregate$ of $\{0,\dots,compositenumber-2\}$ for which $(totalvalue,aggregate)$ is an inversion correspond
to the elements of $\{0, \dots, 3totalvalue-compositenumber+1\}$ congruent to 1 modulo 3, which are
$(3totalvalue-compositenumber+2)/3 = totalvalue - (compositenumber-2)/3$ in number. This contributes a total of
$1 + \cdots + (compositenumber-2)/3 = (compositenumber-2)(compositenumber+1)/18$ inversions.

Summing up, the total number of inversions is $(compositenumber-2)(compositenumber+1)/6$, which is even if and only if
$compositenumber \equiv 3 \pmod{4}$. This proves the claim.

\noindent
\textbf{Second solution} (by Noam Elkies).
Recall that the sign of $stagnation$ (which is $+1$ if $stagnation$ is even and $-1$ if $stagnation$ is odd)
can be computed as
\[
\prod_{0 \leq knownvalue < samevalue < compositenumber} \frac{stagnation(knownvalue) - stagnation(samevalue)}{knownvalue - samevalue}
\]
(because composing $stagnation$ with a transposition changes the sign of the product).
Reducing modulo $compositenumber$, we get a congruence with
\[
\prod_{0 \leq knownvalue < samevalue < compositenumber} \frac{knownvalue^3-samevalue^3}{knownvalue-samevalue} = \prod_{0 \leq knownvalue < samevalue < compositenumber} (knownvalue^2 + knownvalue samevalue + samevalue^2).
\]
It thus suffices to count the number of times each possible value of $knownvalue^2+knownvalue samevalue+samevalue^2$ occurs.
Each nonzero value $variabledata$ modulo $compositenumber$ occurs $compositenumber+1$ times as $knownvalue^2+knownvalue samevalue+samevalue^2$ with $0 \leq knownvalue, samevalue < compositenumber$
and hence $(compositenumber + \magnitude(variabledata/3))/2$ times with $0 \leq knownvalue < samevalue < compositenumber$, where $\magnitude$ denotes the quadratic character
modulo $compositenumber$. Since $compositenumber \equiv 2 \pmod{3}$, by the law of quadratic reciprocity we have
$\magnitude(-3) = +1$, so $\magnitude(variabledata/3) = \magnitude(-variabledata)$.
It thus remains to evaluate the product $\prod_{variabledata=1}^{compositenumber-1} variabledata^{(compositenumber+\magnitude(-variabledata))/2}$ modulo $compositenumber$.

If $compositenumber \equiv 3 \pmod{4}$, this is easy: each factor is a quadratic residue (this is clear if $variabledata$ is a residue,
and otherwise $\magnitude(-variabledata) = +1$ so $compositenumber+\magnitude(-variabledata)$ is divisible by $4$) and $-1$ is not, so we must get $+1$
modulo $compositenumber$.

If $compositenumber \equiv 1 \pmod{4}$, we must do more work: we
choose a primitive root $destroyer$ modulo $compositenumber$ and rewrite the product as
\[
\prod_{totalvalue=0}^{compositenumber-2} destroyer^{totalvalue(compositenumber+(-1)^{totalvalue})/2}.
\]
The sum of the exponents, split into sums over $totalvalue$ odd and $totalvalue$ even, gives
\[
\sum_{aggregate=0}^{(compositenumber-3)/2} \left( aggregate(compositenumber+1) + \frac{(2aggregate+1)(compositenumber-1)}{2}\right)
\]
which simplifies to
\[
\frac{(compositenumber-3)(compositenumber-1)(compositenumber+1)}{8} + \frac{(compositenumber-1)^3}{8} = \frac{compositenumber-1}{2} \left( \frac{compositenumber^2 - 1}{2} - compositenumber \right).
\]
Hence the product we are trying to evaluate is congruent to $destroyer^{(compositenumber-1)/2} \equiv -1$ modulo $compositenumber$.

\noindent
\textbf{Third solution} (by Mark van Hoeij).
We compute the parity of $stagnation$ as the parity of the number of cycles of even length
in the cycle decomposition of $stagnation$.
For $knownvalue$ a nonzero residue class modulo $compositenumber$ of multiplicative order $multipleval$, the elements of the orbit of $knownvalue$
under $stagnation$ also have order $multipleval$ (because $multipleval$ divides $compositenumber-1$ and hence is coprime to $3$). Since the group
of nonzero residue classes modulo $compositenumber$ is cyclic of order $compositenumber-1$, the elements of order $multipleval$ fall into
$divisorcount(multipleval)/constantval(multipleval)$ orbits under $stagnation$, where $divisorcount$ is the Euler phi function and $constantval(multipleval)$ is the
multiplicative order of 3 modulo $multipleval$. The parity of $stagnation$ is then the parity of the sum of $divisorcount(multipleval)/constantval(multipleval)$
over all divisors $multipleval$ of $compositenumber-1$ for which $constantval(multipleval)$ is even.

If $multipleval$ is odd, then $divisorcount(multipleval)/constantval(multipleval) = divisorcount(2multipleval)/constantval(2multipleval)$, so the summands corresponding to $multipleval$ and $2multipleval$
coincide. It thus suffices to consider those $multipleval$ divisible by $4$. If $compositenumber \equiv 3 \pmod{4}$, then there are no
such summands, so the sum is trivially even.

If $compositenumber \equiv 1 \pmod{4}$, then $multipleval=4$ contributes a summand of $divisorcount(4)/constantval(4) = 2/2 = 1$.
For each $multipleval$ which is a larger multiple of 4, the group $(\\ZZ/multipleval\\ZZ)^*$ is isomorphic to the product of
$\\ZZ/2\\ZZ$ with another group of even order, so the maximal power of 2 dividing $constantval(multipleval)$ is strictly smaller
than the maximal power of 2 dividing $multipleval$. Hence $divisorcount(multipleval)/constantval(multipleval)$ is even, and so the overall sum is odd.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark.}
Note that the second proof uses quadratic reciprocity, whereas the first and third proofs are similar to several classical
proofs of quadratic reciprocity. Abhinav Kumar notes that the problem itself is a special case
of the Duke-Hopkins quadratic reciprocity law for abelian groups (Quadratic reciprocity in a finite group,
\textit{Amer. Math. Monthly} \textbf{112} (2005), 251--256; see also
\url{http://math.uga.edu/~pete/morequadrec.pdf}).


\end{itemize}
\end{document}

garbled_string.question:
Let $jzwimnve$ be an odd prime number such that $jzwimnve \equiv 2 \pmod{3}$. Define a permutation $\wcevblas$ of the
residue classes modulo $jzwimnve$ by $\wcevblas(vldgkseo) \equiv vldgkseo^3 \pmod{jzwimnve}$. Show that $\wcevblas$ is an even permutation
if and only if $jzwimnve \equiv 3 \pmod{4}$.
garbled_string.solution:
\textbf{First solution.}
Since fixed points do not affect the signature of a permutation, we may
ignore the residue class of $0$ and consider $\wcevblas$ as a permutation on the
nonzero residue classes modulo $jzwimnve$. These form a cyclic group of order $jzwimnve-1$,
so the signature of $\wcevblas$ is also the signature of multiplication by 3 as
a permutation $\ikxqumrt$ of the residue classes modulo $jzwimnve-1$. If we identify
these classes with the integers $0,\dots,jzwimnve-2$, then the signature equals
the parity of the number of \emph{inversions}: these are the pairs $(hbtciosj,qmrfayzn)$
with $0 \leq hbtciosj < qmrfayzn \leq jzwimnve-2$ for which $\ikxqumrt(hbtciosj) > \ikxqumrt(qmrfayzn)$. We may write
\[
\ikxqumrt(hbtciosj) = 3hbtciosj - (jzwimnve-1) \left\lfloor \frac{3hbtciosj}{jzwimnve-1} \right\rfloor
\]
from which we see that $(hbtciosj,qmrfayzn)$ cannot be an inversion unless $\left\lfloor \frac{3qmrfayzn}{jzwimnve-1} \right\rfloor > \left\lfloor \frac{3hbtciosj}{jzwimnve-1} \right\rfloor$. In particular, we only obtain inversions when $hbtciosj < 2(jzwimnve-1)/3$.

If $hbtciosj < (jzwimnve-1)/3$, the elements $qmrfayzn$ of $\{0,\dots,jzwimnve-2\}$ for which $(hbtciosj,qmrfayzn)$ is an inversion correspond to the elements of $\{0,\dots,3hbtciosj\}$ which are not multiples of $3$, which are $2hbtciosj$ in number.
This contributes a total of $0 + 2 + \cdots + 2(jzwimnve-2)/3 = (jzwimnve-2)(jzwimnve+1)/9$ inversions.

If $(jzwimnve-1)/3 < hbtciosj < 2(jzwimnve-1)/3$, the elements $qmrfayzn$ of $\{0,\dots,jzwimnve-2\}$ for which $(hbtciosj,qmrfayzn)$ is an inversion correspond
to the elements of $\{0, \dots, 3hbtciosj-jzwimnve+1\}$ congruent to 1 modulo 3, which are
$(3hbtciosj-jzwimnve+2)/3 = hbtciosj - (jzwimnve-2)/3$ in number. This contributes a total of
$1 + \cdots + (jzwimnve-2)/3 = (jzwimnve-2)(jzwimnve+1)/18$ inversions.

Summing up, the total number of inversions is $(jzwimnve-2)(jzwimnve+1)/6$, which is even if and only if
$jzwimnve \equiv 3 \pmod{4}$. This proves the claim.

\noindent
\textbf{Second solution} (by Noam Elkies).
Recall that the sign of $\wcevblas$ (which is $+1$ if $\wcevblas$ is even and $-1$ if $\wcevblas$ is odd)
can be computed as
\[
\prod_{0 \leq vldgkseo < rtspnchu < jzwimnve} \frac{\wcevblas(vldgkseo) - \wcevblas(rtspnchu)}{vldgkseo - rtspnchu}
\]
(because composing $\wcevblas$ with a transposition changes the sign of the product).
Reducing modulo $jzwimnve$, we get a congruence with
\[
\prod_{0 \leq vldgkseo < rtspnchu < jzwimnve} \frac{vldgkseo^3-rtspnchu^3}{vldgkseo-rtspnchu} = \prod_{0 \leq vldgkseo < rtspnchu < jzwimnve} (vldgkseo^2 + vldgkseo\,rtspnchu + rtspnchu^2).
\]
It thus suffices to count the number of times each possible value of $vldgkseo^2+vldgkseo\,rtspnchu+rtspnchu^2$ occurs.
Each nonzero value $zdqmxple$ modulo $jzwimnve$ occurs $jzwimnve+1$ times as $vldgkseo^2+vldgkseo\,rtspnchu+rtspnchu^2$ with $0 \leq vldgkseo, rtspnchu < jzwimnve$
and hence $(jzwimnve + \npgfrlke(zdqmxple/3))/2$ times with $0 \leq vldgkseo < rtspnchu < jzwimnve$, where $\npgfrlke$ denotes the quadratic character
modulo $jzwimnve$. Since $jzwimnve \equiv 2 \pmod{3}$, by the law of quadratic reciprocity we have
$\npgfrlke(-3) = +1$, so $\npgfrlke(zdqmxple/3) = \npgfrlke(-zdqmxple)$.
It thus remains to evaluate the product $\prod_{zdqmxple=1}^{jzwimnve-1} zdqmxple^{(jzwimnve+\npgfrlke(-zdqmxple))/2}$ modulo $jzwimnve$.

If $jzwimnve \equiv 3 \pmod{4}$, this is easy: each factor is a quadratic residue (this is clear if $zdqmxple$ is a residue,
and otherwise $\npgfrlke(-zdqmxple) = +1$ so $jzwimnve+\npgfrlke(-zdqmxple)$ is divisible by $4$) and $-1$ is not, so we must get $+1$
modulo $jzwimnve$.

If $jzwimnve \equiv 1 \pmod{4}$, we must do more work: we
choose a primitive root $rktoasfh$ modulo $jzwimnve$ and rewrite the product as
\[
\prod_{hbtciosj=0}^{jzwimnve-2} rktoasfh^{\,hbtciosj(jzwimnve+(-1)^{hbtciosj})/2}.
\]
The sum of the exponents, split into sums over $hbtciosj$ odd and $hbtciosj$ even, gives
\[
\sum_{qmrfayzn=0}^{(jzwimnve-3)/2} \left( qmrfayzn(jzwimnve+1) + \frac{(2qmrfayzn+1)(jzwimnve-1)}{2}\right)
\]
which simplifies to
\[
\frac{(jzwimnve-3)(jzwimnve-1)(jzwimnve+1)}{8} + \frac{(jzwimnve-1)^3}{8} = \frac{jzwimnve-1}{2} \left( \frac{jzwimnve^2 - 1}{2} - jzwimnve \right).
\]
Hence the product we are trying to evaluate is congruent to $rktoasfh^{(jzwimnve-1)/2} \equiv -1$ modulo $jzwimnve$.

\noindent
\textbf{Third solution} (by Mark van Hoeij).
We compute the parity of $\wcevblas$ as the parity of the number of cycles of even length
in the cycle decomposition of $\wcevblas$.
For $vldgkseo$ a nonzero residue class modulo $jzwimnve$ of multiplicative order $enaxuvwr$, the elements of the orbit of $vldgkseo$
under $\wcevblas$ also have order $enaxuvwr$ (because $enaxuvwr$ divides $jzwimnve-1$ and hence is coprime to $3$). Since the group
of nonzero residue classes modulo $jzwimnve$ is cyclic of order $jzwimnve-1$, the elements of order $enaxuvwr$ fall into
$\ycldoepr(enaxuvwr)/ublypqdz(enaxuvwr)$ orbits under $\wcevblas$, where $\ycldoepr$ is the Euler phi function and $ublypqdz(enaxuvwr)$ is the
multiplicative order of 3 modulo $enaxuvwr$. The parity of $\wcevblas$ is then the parity of the sum of $\ycldoepr(enaxuvwr)/ublypqdz(enaxuvwr)$
over all divisors $enaxuvwr$ of $jzwimnve-1$ for which $ublypqdz(enaxuvwr)$ is even.

If $enaxuvwr$ is odd, then $\ycldoepr(enaxuvwr)/ublypqdz(enaxuvwr) = \ycldoepr(2enaxuvwr)/ublypqdz(2enaxuvwr)$, so the summands corresponding to $enaxuvwr$ and $2enaxuvwr$
coincide. It thus suffices to consider those $enaxuvwr$ divisible by $4$. If $jzwimnve \equiv 3 \pmod{4}$, then there are no
such summands, so the sum is trivially even.

If $jzwimnve \equiv 1 \pmod{4}$, then $enaxuvwr=4$ contributes a summand of $\ycldoepr(4)/ublypqdz(4) = 2/2 = 1$.
For each $enaxuvwr$ which is a larger multiple of 4, the group $(\ZZ/enaxuvwr\ZZ)^*$ is isomorphic to the product of
$\ZZ/2\ZZ$ with another group of even order, so the maximal power of 2 dividing $ublypqdz(enaxuvwr)$ is strictly smaller
than the maximal power of 2 dividing $enaxuvwr$. Hence $\ycldoepr(enaxuvwr)/ublypqdz(enaxuvwr)$ is even, and so the overall sum is odd.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark.}
Note that the second proof uses quadratic reciprocity, whereas the first and third proofs are similar to several classical
proofs of quadratic reciprocity. Abhinav Kumar notes that the problem itself is a special case
of the Duke-Hopkins quadratic reciprocity law for abelian groups (Quadratic reciprocity in a finite group,
\textit{Amer. Math. Monthly} \textbf{112} (2005), 251--256; see also
\url{http://math.uga.edu/~pete/morequadrec.pdf}).

kernel_variant.question:
Let $p$ be an odd prime with \(p\equiv 2\pmod 5\).  We define a permutation \(\pi\) of the residue classes mod $p$ by
\[\pi(x)\;\equiv\;x^{\,5}\pmod p.\]
Show that \(\pi\) is always an \\emph{even} permutation; that is, its sign is $+1$ for every prime $p$ satisfying the above congruence.
kernel_variant.solution:
Solution.  Let p be an odd prime with p\equiv 2 (mod 5), and set n=p-1.  We wish to show that the permutation \pi  on F_p defined by \pi (x)=x^5 is even.  Since 0\to 0 is fixed, we may ignore it and identify the remaining p-1 nonzero residues with the integers modulo n via a choice of generator g of F_p^*.  Under this identification \pi  becomes the map \sigma (i)=5i (mod n) on {0,1,\ldots ,n-1}.  The sign of \sigma  equals (-1)^Inv, where Inv is the number of inversions, i.e.\, pairs 0\leq i<j\leq n-1 with \sigma (i)>\sigma (j).  A well-known lemma (often proved by pairing i with n-i) states:  

   If gcd(k,n)=1, then  Inv = \sum _{i=1}^{n-1}\lfloor k i/n\rfloor .

In our case k=5 and n=p-1.  Since p\equiv 2 (mod 5), write p-1=5q+1, so gcd(5,n)=1.  Hence

   Inv = \sum _{i=1}^{n-1}\lfloor 5i/n\rfloor  = (5-1)(n-1)/2 = 4\cdot (5q)/2 = 10q,

which is even.  Therefore sgn(\sigma )= (-1)^Inv = +1, and so \pi  is an even permutation of F_p.  This completes the proof.

core_steps: Delete the fixed class 0 and view x ↦ x^3 as multiplication-by-3 on the cyclic group of order p−1.
Translate permutation sign to the sign of the index map i ↦ 3i mod (p−1).
Express that sign as the parity of the inversion count of the map on {0,…,p−2}.
Compute inversions by splitting the index set at multiples of (p−1)/3 and summing explicit counts.
Show total inversions ≡ (p−2)(p+1)/6 (mod 2), hence even ⇔ p ≡ 3 (mod 4).

slot1.description: Exponent used in the map x ↦ x^k (currently k=3). Any k coprime to p−1 would allow the same chain of arguments.

slot2.description: Arithmetic condition ensuring that x ↦ x^k is a bijection (currently gcd(k,p−1)=1 expressed as p ≡ 2 (mod 3)).

slot3.description: Break points (p−1)/k, 2(p−1)/k, … used to classify inversions; they scale with the chosen exponent.

slot4.description: Final congruence describing when the permutation is even; its exact form depends on k but not on the method.
